title: SEGMENT 2 — 1990: the audacity of proposing to sequence three billion bases

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — 1990: the audacity of proposing to sequence three billion bases
I want you to time-travel with me to 1985.
Reagan is president, the personal computer revolution is just starting to penetrate biology labs, and the standard way you sequenced a piece of DNA was this.

You would take your DNA fragment, you would set up a Sanger sequencing reaction in four tubes — one for each base, A, C, G, T — you would run the products on a polyacrylamide gel that was the size of a small window, you would expose the gel to X-ray film overnight, and the next morning you would stand at a light box with a pencil and read off the bases by eye.
A good day, a really good day, was five hundred bases.
A great year for a graduate student was maybe fifty thousand bases — about the size of a single bacterial gene cluster.
Now hold that picture in your head — graduate student, light box, pencil, five hundred bases per gel — and let me give you the number we are trying to reach.
Three billion bases.
Let me make that number physical for you, because three billion is the kind of number that washes over your ear without sticking.
Here is the analogy.

If you printed out the human genome at ten letters per line — A T G C C T A G C T, ten letters, line break, ten more letters — and you used standard book typography, that printout would be a stack of phone books taller than this room.
Floor to ceiling.
Three hundred million lines.
If you started reading at one letter per second, eight hours a day, it would take you about three hundred years to finish.
That is what they proposed to sequence.
And the sequencing method available to them was the graduate-student-and-light-box method, with a few prototype automated machines that were just starting to come online.
The original proposal — and there were several converging proposals in 1985, 1986, 1987 — came from a few different camps.
Robert Sinsheimer at UC Santa Cruz hosted a meeting in 1985 to discuss whether sequencing the human genome was even possible.
Renato Dulbecco, the Nobel laureate, wrote an influential 1986 editorial in Science arguing that to make progress on cancer we needed the complete reference sequence of the human genome.

And Charles DeLisi at the Department of Energy started organizing a federal effort because the DOE had a longstanding interest in the genetic effects of radiation — they had been studying atomic bomb survivors for decades — and they had supercomputers.
The Department of Energy and the National Institutes of Health eventually agreed to jointly lead the U.
S. effort.
The project officially launched on October 1, 1990.

The first director of what became the National Human Genome Research Institute, NHGRI, was James Watson — yes, that Watson, of Watson and Crick — who lasted in the role only until 1992 when he resigned, partly over his opposition to the patenting of gene sequences, an issue that would become central to the Venter rivalry we will get to.
Francis Collins took over from Watson in 1993 and led NHGRI through the entire rest of the project.

Collins is a careful, methodical, deeply religious physician-scientist who had previously co-discovered the cystic fibrosis gene, and he turned out to be exactly the right kind of leader for the kind of slow, grinding, international, politically delicate work that the HGP required.
But — and this is critical — this was never just an American project.
The Wellcome Trust in the UK funded what became the Sanger Centre, now the Wellcome Sanger Institute, under John Sulston.
The French government funded Genoscope under Jean Weissenbach.
Germany, Japan — RIKEN especially — and eventually China through what became BGI all contributed major sequencing centers.
The project was structured as the International Human Genome Sequencing Consortium, with roughly twenty institutions across six countries doing the work in coordinated chunks.
The original timeline was fifteen years, 1990 to 2005.
The original budget was three billion dollars over those fifteen years — roughly one dollar per base, which sounds quaint now but was an aggressive cost target in 1990.
How were they going to do it?
That is the next two segments — the players, then the technology.
But before we leave the audacity of the proposal, I want you to sit with one fact.
In 1990, no organism larger than a small bacterium had been fully sequenced.
The first complete genome of any free-living organism, Haemophilus influenzae, was not sequenced until 1995 — five years into the HGP.
They committed to sequencing three billion bases of human before anybody had ever sequenced two million bases of anything.
That is what audacity looks like.
